title: Math 314: Final Exam Practice (Spring 2026)
description: Comprehensive practice questions covering row equivalence, subspaces, determinants, eigenvalues, orthogonality, and linear transformations.

questions.prompt: True or False: If $A$ and $B$ are row-equivalent matrices, then $\text{col}(A) = \text{col}(B)$.

choices.label: False — row operations preserve the row space, but can change the column space.

questions.explanation: Row operations (like swapping rows) do not generally preserve the column space. For example, $A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ are row-equivalent, but their column spaces are different axes in $\mathbb{R}^2$[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: True or False: For any matrix $A$, $\text{dim}(\text{row}(A)) = \text{dim}(\text{col}(A))$.

choices.label: True — this dimension is known as the rank of the matrix.

questions.explanation: The Rank Theorem states that the dimension of the row space and the column space are always equal to the number of pivot positions in the matrix[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: Consider a $6 \times 6$ matrix $A$ where $\text{dim}(\text{null}(A)) = 1$. What is the dimension of $\text{col}(A)$?
questions.explanation: By the Rank-Nullity Theorem, $\text{rank}(A) + \text{nullity}(A) = n$. Here, $n=6$ and $\text{nullity}(A)=1$, so $\text{rank}(A) = 6 - 1 = 5$[cite: 5].

choices.label: No — an invertible matrix must have a nullity of zero.

questions.explanation: According to the Invertible Matrix Theorem, a square matrix is invertible if and only if its null space contains only the zero vector ($\text{dim}(\text{null}(A)) = 0$)[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: If matrix $C$ has determinant 3 and matrix $D$ has determinant 4 (both $3 \times 3$), what is the determinant of the product $CD$?
questions.explanation: The determinant of a product is the product of the determinants: $\det(CD) = \det(C)\det(D) = 3 \times 4 = 12$[cite: 5].

questions.prompt: Suppose $A$ is a diagonalizable $4 \times 4$ matrix with eigenvalues 1, 1, 2, and 5. Compute the determinant of $A$.
questions.explanation: The determinant of a matrix is equal to the product of its eigenvalues: $1 \times 1 \times 2 \times 5 = 10$[cite: 5].

choices.label: No — $A\mathbf{v}$ is not a multiple of $\mathbf{v}$.

questions.explanation: $A\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix} = 5 \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$. Since the output is a scalar multiple of the input, $\mathbf{v}$ is an eigenvector[cite: 5].
